macro_segments.title: The Sanhedrin's Exile from the Beit HaMikdash and the Cessation of Capital Punishment

micro_segments.title: The forty-year relocation of the Sanhedrin from the Beit HaMikdash to the Khanut and why they could no longer judge capital crimes
The account of Rabbi Yehuda ben Bava granting smicha to five students despite Roman persecution
The Gemara's reinterpretation: the real reason for the Sanhedrin's relocation was the proliferation of murderers and the inability to execute capital punishment

macro_segments.title: Rabbinic Chronology: Periods of Non-Jewish Rule over Israel

micro_segments.title: The timeline of kingdoms: Persian rule (34 years), Greek rule (180 years), and the Hasmonean/Roman rule leading to the destruction of the Beit HaMikdash
The dating formula and the twenty-six-year delay in Roman dominion before actual subjugation of Israel

macro_segments.title: Three Systems of Dating Documents (Shtarot) in Rabbinic Law

micro_segments.title: Introduction to three dating systems: counting from Beriyas Olam (creation), from Churban HaBayis (destruction of the Temple), and Minyan Shtarot (standard document dating)
Explanation of Minyan Shtarot: the standard system used by scribes, beginning 380 years before the Churban HaBayis
Conversion formulas between dating systems: adding or subtracting 20 years to convert between Churban HaBayis and Minyan Shtarot dating
Historical context: Minyan Shtarot is based on the Seleucid Empire (312 BCE), which became the standard dating system throughout the ancient world

macro_segments.title: Rabbinic Cosmology: The Six-Thousand-Year Cycle and Messianic Expectations

micro_segments.title: The Tanna D'vei Eliyahu teaching: the world lasts 6,000 years (2,000 void, 2,000 Torah, 2,000 Messiah era), with the seventh thousand as the eternal world
Dating the beginning of the Torah era: why not from Matan Torah but from Abraham's preaching of monotheism in Charan at age 52
Chronological calculation: determining year 4,000 (end of Torah era, beginning of Messiah era) falls in year 172 CE, about the time of the Tannaim

macro_segments.title: Conversion Between Beriyas Olam and Minyan Shtarot Dating Systems

micro_segments.title: If one knows the Minyan Shtarot date and needs to convert to Beriyas Olam (year 4,000 based) dating: add 48 to the last two digits
The mnemonic aid: the 48 cities of the Levites (Ernet Arbaim u'Shmoneh Ir) help remember the conversion formula
Reverse conversion: if one knows the Beriyas Olam date and needs Minyan Shtarot, subtract 48 from the last two digits

macro_segments.title: Determining the Year of the Shmita Cycle Using Churban HaBayis as a Reference Point

micro_segments.title: The principle: the Churban HaBayis occurred in year 1 of a Shmita cycle; add 1 to the number of years elapsed and divide by 7
Alternative algorithm for calculating Shmita without division: extract hundreds, account for two years per hundred removed, then divide the remainder by 7
Mnemonic for the two-year-per-hundred adjustment: 'two years of famine in the land' (from Genesis)

macro_segments.title: Messianic Calculations and Expectations Around Year 400 After Churban HaBayis

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Chanina's teaching: 400 years after Churban HaBayis, one should not invest in property outside Israel because Messiah should arrive by then
A conflicting baraita sets the messianic deadline at year 4231 from creation (only 3 years later), reconciling different chronological systems

topical_tags.term: Messiah should arrive by 400 years after Churban
Baraita setting messianic deadline at year 4231
Rav Huna son of Rav Yehoshua
